svg.text: Multiplication of Large Integers & Strassen's Matrix Multiplication

g.data-narration: Welcome! We explore efficient algorithms for multiplying large integers and matrices, beating traditional O(n²) and O(n³) complexities.
Traditional school multiplication takes O(n²) time - doubling input size quadruples computation time, creating major bottlenecks.
These inefficiencies impact cryptography, scientific computing, and machine learning where operations involve thousands of digits or matrix elements.
Now we dive deep into the Karatsuba algorithm - the first method to beat quadratic complexity for integer multiplication.
Karatsuba divides each n-digit number into two halves, reducing four recursive multiplications to just three using a clever trick.
The clever insight: compute (a+b)(c+d) once, then subtract ac and bd to get ad+bc without direct multiplication.
We compute three products: ac, bd, and (a+b)(c+d). Then combine them using the formula to get the final result.
Let's multiply 1234 × 5678. Split into (12,34) and (56,78), compute the three products, then combine them.
Karatsuba achieves Θ(n^log₂3) ≈ Θ(n^1.585), much faster than Θ(n²) for large integers. Log base 2 of 3 is approximately 1.585.
Now we explore Strassen's algorithm for matrix multiplication, which reduces 8 recursive multiplications to 7.
Strassen's algorithm multiplies 2×2 matrices using 7 clever products instead of 8, achieving O(n^log₂7) ≈ O(n^2.807) complexity.
The seven Strassen products use clever combinations of matrix elements to reduce one multiplication. Memorize these formulas!
After computing P1-P7, we combine them to get the result matrix C. Four formulas give us all four elements of C.
Let's verify Strassen with A=|1 2| and B=|5 6|. Compute P1-P7, then combine to get C = |19 22|.
Both algorithms have overhead that makes them slower for small inputs. Break-even points: Karatsuba at ~10-20 digits, Strassen at ~32×32 matrices.
Key takeaway: divide-and-conquer reduces multiplications - Karatsuba to 3 (Θ(n^1.585)), Strassen to 7 (Θ(n^2.807)). Both beat traditional methods for large inputs.
